revision.text:
{{Infobox ancient site | name = Kotani Kofun | image = [[File:Kotani Kofun, entrance.jpg|280px]]
Entrance to the stone chamber | Alternate name = | Affiliation = | location = [[Toriya-cho]], [[Kashihara]], [[Nara Prefecture]] | Latitude Degrees = 34 | Latitude Minutes = 28 | Latitude Seconds = 29.03 | Longitude Degrees = 135 | Longitude Minutes = 47 | Longitude Seconds = 5.52 | ISO = JP-29 | Shape = Unknown | Size = Length: 30 m
Height: 8 m | Burial Facility = Double-winged [[yokoana-style stone chamber]] (Iwayayama style)
(Contains a hewn [[house-shaped sarcophagus]]) | Excavated Items = | Satellite Tombs = | Construction Period = [[7th century]] | Buried Figure = | Mausoleum = | Historic Site = Designated historic site of Nara Prefecture: "Kotani Kofun" | Tangible Cultural Property = | Notes = | Map = Japan Nara | Icon = | Label = Kotani Kofun | Label Position = top }} '''Kotani Kofun''' (小谷古墳) is a [[kofun]] (ancient burial mound) located in {{ill|Kashihara|ja|橿原市}}, [[Nara Prefecture]]. It is designated as a Nara Prefectural Historic Site. == Overview == The Kotani Kofun is situated on the southern slope of a ridge extending northeast from Kaibukiyama at the southern edge of the [[Nara Basin]]Historical Site Explanation Board.. The mound is part of a cluster of eight kofun in the area, positioned at the easternmost point of the group. The mound has suffered significant erosion, and no archaeological excavations have been conducted. The mound's shape is uncertain but is hypothesized to be either circular or square. It measures approximately 30 meters in length and 8 meters in height. The burial facility consists of a {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chambers|ja|横穴式石室}} with double sidewalls, opening to the south. The stone chamber is constructed with well-cut granite blocks and features a sophisticated design similar to the {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} in {{ill|Asuka|ja|明日香村}}, making it an example of the "Iwayayama-style stone chamber." Within the chamber lies a {{ill|house-shaped sarcophagus|ja|家形石棺}} carved from [[Hyogo Prefecture]]'s Tatsuyama Stone (stratified hyaloclastite)Tatsuyama Stone has been identified as rhyolitic stratified hyaloclastite (water-cooled pyroclastic rock) based on recent studies. See [https://web.pref.hyogo.lg.jp/sr09/jibasan/31.html Tatsuyama Stone (Hyogo Prefectural Website)] for details.. The grave goods have not been confirmed due to the lack of excavation. The mound dates to the [[7th century]] during the late [[Kofun period]]. It is recognized as one of the representative terminal period kofun in Nara Prefecture, featuring a stone chamber comparable in scale to those used for royal tombs. While the identity of the interred is unknown, the burial is speculated to belong to an influential aristocratic family, possibly of royal lineage. In the [[Edo period]], it was suggested to be the tomb of [[Empress Saimei]]. The site was designated as a Nara Prefectural Historic Site in [[1975]] (Showa 50). Currently, access to the interior of the stone chamber is restricted. Nearby is the Kotani East Kofun (whose stone chamber remains unopened), and it has been proposed that the two kofun form a twin burial mound pairKunihiko Kawakami, *Asuka Excavation Stories*, Fusosha, 2004, pp. 70-72.. == Burial Facility == [[File:Kotani Kofun, 3D-B-2.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Bird's-eye view of the stone chamber.}}]] [[File:Kotani Kofun, 3D-N-2.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Detailed layout of the stone chamber.}}]] [[File:Kotani Kofun, sekkan 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Layout of the carved sarcophagus.}}]] The burial facility comprises a double sidewall corridor-style stone chamber, opening to the south. Its dimensions are as follows: * '''Total length:''' Approximately 11.6 meters * '''Main chamber:''' Length ~5 meters, Width ~2.8 meters, Height ~2.7 meters * '''Entrance corridor:''' Length ~6.45 meters, Width ~1.9 meters, Height ~1.8 meters The chamber is constructed from large, cut granite blocks with meticulous craftsmanship. Both the rear and side walls of the main chamber consist of two layers, and the seams are sealed with plaster. A single large stone serves as the ceiling. The main chamber floor is elevated one step above the corridor. Inside the main chamber lies a house-shaped sarcophagus carved from Tatsuyama Stone[https://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/kotani.html Kotani Kofun] (Kashihara City "Kashihara Visit Navi").. The lid, almost semicylindrical in shape, lacks suspension lugs, indicating a newer style within house-shaped sarcophagi. The sarcophagus measures 2.4 meters in length, 1.16 meters in width, and 0.82 meters in height. The chamber shares features with the Iwayayama Kofun, classified as "Iwayayama-style." While the main chambers are similar in size, the corridor of Kotani Kofun is shorter. The stone chamber is also nearly identical in design to the {{ill|Minezuka Kofun|ja|峯塚古墳}} in {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}, suggesting a shift from local clan control of stonework to central administration under the imperial court during the terminal kofun periodKunihiko Kawakami, *Asuka Excavation Stories*, Fusosha, 2004, pp. 66-69.. Minezuka Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|Reference: Bird's-eye view of the Minezuka Kofun stone chamber Kotani Kofun, genshitsu-1-2.jpg|Main chamber (view toward the rear wall) Kotani Kofun, sekkan-2.jpg|Carved house-shaped sarcophagus inside the main chamber Kotani Kofun, genshitsu-2-2.jpg|Main chamber (view toward the entrance) Kotani Kofun, sendou-2-2.jpg|Entrance corridor (view toward the entrance) Kotani Kofun, sendou-1-2.jpg|Entrance corridor (view toward the main chamber) Kotani Kofun, entrance-2.jpg|Entrance == Cultural Properties == === Nara Prefectural Cultural Properties === * '''Historic Site''' ** Kotani Kofun: Designated on March 31, 1975 (Showa 50){{PDFlink|[http://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/143530/kenshiitiran.pdf Nara Prefecture Cultural Property List]}} (Nara Prefectural Website).. == References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * Historical site explanation board (installed by the Kashihara City Board of Education). * {{Cite book|year=1981|chapter=Kotani Kofun|title=Nara Prefecture Place names|series=[[Japanese Historical Place name Series]] 30|publisher=[[Heibonsha]]|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|Kotani Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Kotani Kofun}} * [https://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/kotani.html Kotani Kofun] - Kashihara City "Kashihara Visit Navi" [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Kofun in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:History of Kashihara]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Nara Prefectural Historic Sites]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:pp]][[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Kodani Kofun]][[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:pp]][[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Kodani Kofun]][[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Kodani Kofun]][[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Kodani Kofun]][[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:pp]][[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Kodani Kofun]][[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] {{Kofun navbar}} [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Kodani Kofun]][[ja:小谷古墳]]

{{Infobox ancient site | name = Kotani Kofun | image = [[File:Kotani Kofun, entrance.jpg|280px]]
Entrance to the stone chamber | Alternate name = | Affiliation = | location = [[Toriya-cho]], [[Kashihara]], [[Nara Prefecture]] | Latitude Degrees = 34 | Latitude Minutes = 28 | Latitude Seconds = 29.03 | Longitude Degrees = 135 | Longitude Minutes = 47 | Longitude Seconds = 5.52 | ISO = JP-29 | Shape = Unknown | Size = Length: 30 m
Height: 8 m | Burial Facility = Double-winged [[yokoana-style stone chamber]] (Iwayayama style)
(Contains a hewn [[house-shaped sarcophagus]]) | Excavated Items = | Satellite Tombs = | Construction Period = [[7th century]] | Buried Figure = | Mausoleum = | Historic Site = Designated historic site of Nara Prefecture: "Kotani Kofun" | Tangible Cultural Property = | Notes = | Map = Japan Nara | Icon = | Label = Kotani Kofun | Label Position = top }} '''Kotani Kofun''' (小谷古墳) is a [[kofun]] (ancient burial mound) located in {{ill|Kashihara|ja|橿原市}}, [[Nara Prefecture]]. It is designated as a Nara Prefectural Historic Site. == Overview == The Kotani Kofun is situated on the southern slope of a ridge extending northeast from Kaibukiyama at the southern edge of the [[Nara Basin]]Historical Site Explanation Board.. The mound is part of a cluster of eight kofun in the area, positioned at the easternmost point of the group. The mound has suffered significant erosion, and no archaeological excavations have been conducted. The mound's shape is uncertain but is hypothesized to be either circular or square. It measures approximately 30 meters in length and 8 meters in height. The burial facility consists of a {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chambers|ja|横穴式石室}} with double sidewalls, opening to the south. The stone chamber is constructed with well-cut granite blocks and features a sophisticated design similar to the {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} in {{ill|Asuka|ja|明日香村}}, making it an example of the "Iwayayama-style stone chamber." Within the chamber lies a {{ill|house-shaped sarcophagus|ja|家形石棺}} carved from [[Hyogo Prefecture]]'s Tatsuyama Stone (stratified hyaloclastite)Tatsuyama Stone has been identified as rhyolitic stratified hyaloclastite (water-cooled pyroclastic rock) based on recent studies. See [https://web.pref.hyogo.lg.jp/sr09/jibasan/31.html Tatsuyama Stone (Hyogo Prefectural Website)] for details.. The grave goods have not been confirmed due to the lack of excavation. The mound dates to the [[7th century]] during the late [[Kofun period]]. It is recognized as one of the representative terminal period kofun in Nara Prefecture, featuring a stone chamber comparable in scale to those used for royal tombs. While the identity of the interred is unknown, the burial is speculated to belong to an influential aristocratic family, possibly of royal lineage. In the [[Edo period]], it was suggested to be the tomb of [[Empress Saimei]]. The site was designated as a Nara Prefectural Historic Site in [[1975]] (Showa 50). Currently, access to the interior of the stone chamber is restricted. Nearby is the Kotani East Kofun (whose stone chamber remains unopened), and it has been proposed that the two kofun form a twin burial mound pairKunihiko Kawakami, *Asuka Excavation Stories*, Fusosha, 2004, pp. 70-72.. == Burial Facility == [[File:Kotani Kofun, 3D-B-2.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Bird's-eye view of the stone chamber.}}]] [[File:Kotani Kofun, 3D-N-2.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Detailed layout of the stone chamber.}}]] [[File:Kotani Kofun, sekkan 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Layout of the carved sarcophagus.}}]] The burial facility comprises a double sidewall corridor-style stone chamber, opening to the south. Its dimensions are as follows: * '''Total length:''' Approximately 11.6 meters * '''Main chamber:''' Length ~5 meters, Width ~2.8 meters, Height ~2.7 meters * '''Entrance corridor:''' Length ~6.45 meters, Width ~1.9 meters, Height ~1.8 meters The chamber is constructed from large, cut granite blocks with meticulous craftsmanship. Both the rear and side walls of the main chamber consist of two layers, and the seams are sealed with plaster. A single large stone serves as the ceiling. The main chamber floor is elevated one step above the corridor. Inside the main chamber lies a house-shaped sarcophagus carved from Tatsuyama Stone[https://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/kotani.html Kotani Kofun] (Kashihara City "Kashihara Visit Navi").. The lid, almost semicylindrical in shape, lacks suspension lugs, indicating a newer style within house-shaped sarcophagi. The sarcophagus measures 2.4 meters in length, 1.16 meters in width, and 0.82 meters in height. The chamber shares features with the Iwayayama Kofun, classified as "Iwayayama-style." While the main chambers are similar in size, the corridor of Kotani Kofun is shorter. The stone chamber is also nearly identical in design to the {{ill|Minezuka Kofun|ja|峯塚古墳}} in {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}, suggesting a shift from local clan control of stonework to central administration under the imperial court during the terminal kofun periodKunihiko Kawakami, *Asuka Excavation Stories*, Fusosha, 2004, pp. 66-69.. Minezuka Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|Reference: Bird's-eye view of the Minezuka Kofun stone chamber Kotani Kofun, genshitsu-1-2.jpg|Main chamber (view toward the rear wall) Kotani Kofun, sekkan-2.jpg|Carved house-shaped sarcophagus inside the main chamber Kotani Kofun, genshitsu-2-2.jpg|Main chamber (view toward the entrance) Kotani Kofun, sendou-2-2.jpg|Entrance corridor (view toward the entrance) Kotani Kofun, sendou-1-2.jpg|Entrance corridor (view toward the main chamber) Kotani Kofun, entrance-2.jpg|Entrance == Cultural Properties == === Nara Prefectural Cultural Properties === * '''Historic Site''' ** Kotani Kofun: Designated on March 31, 1975 (Showa 50){{PDFlink|[http://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/143530/kenshiitiran.pdf Nara Prefecture Cultural Property List]}} (Nara Prefectural Website).. == References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * Historical site explanation board (installed by the Kashihara City Board of Education). * {{Cite book|year=1981|chapter=Kotani Kofun|title=Nara Prefecture Place names|series=[[Japanese Historical Place name Series]] 30|publisher=[[Heibonsha]]|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|Kotani Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Kotani Kofun}} * [https://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/kotani.html Kotani Kofun] - {{Infobox ancient site | name = Kotani Kofun | image = [[File:Kotani Kofun, entrance.jpg|280px]]
Entrance to the stone chamber | Alternate name = | Affiliation = | location = [[Toriya-cho]], [[Kashihara]], [[Nara Prefecture]] | Latitude Degrees = 34 | Latitude Minutes = 28 | Latitude Seconds = 29.03 | Longitude Degrees = 135 | Longitude Minutes = 47 | Longitude Seconds = 5.52 | ISO = JP-29 | Shape = Unknown | Size = Length: 30 m
Height: 8 m | Burial Facility = Double-winged [[yokoana-style stone chamber]] (Iwayayama style)
(Contains a hewn [[house-shaped sarcophagus]]) | Excavated Items = | Satellite Tombs = | Construction Period = [[7th century]] | Buried Figure = | Mausoleum = | Historic Site = Designated historic site of Nara Prefecture: "Kotani Kofun" | Tangible Cultural Property = | Notes = | Map = Japan Nara | Icon = | Label = Kotani Kofun | Label Position = top }} '''Kotani Kofun''' (小谷古墳) is a [[kofun]] (ancient burial mound) located in {{ill|Kashihara|ja|橿原市}}, [[Nara Prefecture]]. It is designated as a Nara Prefectural Historic Site. == Overview == The Kotani Kofun is situated on the southern slope of a ridge extending northeast from Kaibukiyama at the southern edge of the [[Nara Basin]]Historical Site Explanation Board.. The mound is part of a cluster of eight kofun in the area, positioned at the easternmost point of the group. The mound has suffered significant erosion, and no archaeological excavations have been conducted. The mound's shape is uncertain but is hypothesized to be either circular or square. It measures approximately 30 meters in length and 8 meters in height. The burial facility consists of a {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chambers|ja|横穴式石室}} with double sidewalls, opening to the south. The stone chamber is constructed with well-cut granite blocks and features a sophisticated design similar to the {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} in {{ill|Asuka|ja|明日香村}}, making it an example of the "Iwayayama-style stone chamber." Within the chamber lies a {{ill|house-shaped sarcophagus|ja|家形石棺}} carved from [[Hyogo Prefecture]]'s Tatsuyama Stone (stratified hyaloclastite)Tatsuyama Stone has been identified as rhyolitic stratified hyaloclastite (water-cooled pyroclastic rock) based on recent studies. See [https://web.pref.hyogo.lg.jp/sr09/jibasan/31.html Tatsuyama Stone (Hyogo Prefectural Website)] for details.. The grave goods have not been confirmed due to the lack of excavation. The mound dates to the [[7th century]] during the late [[Kofun period]]. It is recognized as one of the representative terminal period kofun in Nara Prefecture, featuring a stone chamber comparable in scale to those used for royal tombs. While the identity of the interred is unknown, the burial is speculated to belong to an influential aristocratic family, possibly of royal lineage. In the [[Edo period]], it was suggested to be the tomb of [[Empress Saimei]]. The site was designated as a Nara Prefectural Historic Site in [[1975]] (Showa 50). Currently, access to the interior of the stone chamber is restricted. Nearby is the Kotani East Kofun (whose stone chamber remains unopened), and it has been proposed that the two kofun form a twin burial mound pairKunihiko Kawakami, *Asuka Excavation Stories*, Fusosha, 2004, pp. 70-72.. == Burial Facility == [[File:Kotani Kofun, 3D-B-2.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Bird's-eye view of the stone chamber.}}]] [[File:Kotani Kofun, 3D-N-2.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Detailed layout of the stone chamber.}}]] [[File:Kotani Kofun, sekkan 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Layout of the carved sarcophagus.}}]] The burial facility comprises a double sidewall corridor-style stone chamber, opening to the south. Its dimensions are as follows: * '''Total length:''' Approximately 11.6 meters * '''Main chamber:''' Length ~5 meters, Width ~2.8 meters, Height ~2.7 meters * '''Entrance corridor:''' Length ~6.45 meters, Width ~1.9 meters, Height ~1.8 meters The chamber is constructed from large, cut granite blocks with meticulous craftsmanship. Both the rear and side walls of the main chamber consist of two layers, and the seams are sealed with plaster. A single large stone serves as the ceiling. The main chamber floor is elevated one step above the corridor. Inside the main chamber lies a house-shaped sarcophagus carved from Tatsuyama Stone[https://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/kotani.html Kotani Kofun] (Kashihara City "Kashihara Visit Navi").. The lid, almost semicylindrical in shape, lacks suspension lugs, indicating a newer style within house-shaped sarcophagi. The sarcophagus measures 2.4 meters in length, 1.16 meters in width, and 0.82 meters in height. The chamber shares features with the Iwayayama Kofun, classified as "Iwayayama-style." While the main chambers are similar in size, the corridor of Kotani Kofun is shorter. The stone chamber is also nearly identical in design to the {{ill|Minezuka Kofun|ja|峯塚古墳}} in {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}, suggesting a shift from local clan control of stonework to central administration under the imperial court during the terminal kofun periodKunihiko Kawakami, *Asuka Excavation Stories*, Fusosha, 2004, pp. 66-69.. Minezuka Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|Reference: Bird's-eye view of the Minezuka Kofun stone chamber Kotani Kofun, genshitsu-1-2.jpg|Main chamber (view toward the rear wall) Kotani Kofun, sekkan-2.jpg|Carved house-shaped sarcophagus inside the main chamber Kotani Kofun, genshitsu-2-2.jpg|Main chamber (view toward the entrance) Kotani Kofun, sendou-2-2.jpg|Entrance corridor (view toward the entrance) Kotani Kofun, sendou-1-2.jpg|Entrance corridor (view toward the main chamber) Kotani Kofun, entrance-2.jpg|Entrance == Cultural Properties == === Nara Prefectural Cultural Properties === * '''Historic Site''' ** Kotani Kofun: Designated on March 31, 1975 (Showa 50){{PDFlink|[http://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/143530/kenshiitiran.pdf Nara Prefecture Cultural Property List]}} (Nara Prefectural Website).. == References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * Historical site explanation board (installed by the Kashihara City Board of Education). * {{Cite book|year=1981|chapter=Kotani Kofun|title=Nara Prefecture Place names|series=[[Japanese Historical Place name Series]] 30|publisher=[[Heibonsha]]|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|Kotani Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Kotani Kofun}} * [https://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/kotani.html Kotani Kofun] - The sarcophagus measures 2.4 meters in length, 1.16 meters in width, and 0.82 meters in height. The chamber shares features with the Iwayayama Kofun, classified as "Iwayayama-style." While the main chambers are similar in size, the corridor of Kotani Kofun is shorter. The stone chamber is also nearly identical in design to the {{ill|Minezuka Kofun|ja|峯塚古墳}} in {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}, suggesting a shift from local clan control of stonework to central administration under the imperial court during the terminal kofun periodKunihiko Kawakami, *Asuka Excavation Stories*, Fusosha, 2004, pp. 66-69.. house-shaped sarcophagus inside the main chamber Kotani Kofun, genshitsu-2-2.jpg|Main chamber (view toward the entrance) Kotani Kofun, sendou-2-2.jpg|Entrance corridor (view toward the entrance) Site''' ** Kotani Kofun: Designated on March 31, 1975 (Showa 50){{PDFlink|[http://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/143530/kenshiitiran.pdf Nara Prefecture Cultural Property List]}} (Nara Prefectural Website).. revision.comment: Adding [[Category:横穴式石室]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:横穴式石室]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Infobox ancient site | name = Kotani Kofun | image = [[File:Kotani Kofun, entrance.jpg|280px]]
Entrance to the stone chamber | Alternate name = | Affiliation = | location = [[Toriya-cho]], [[Kashihara]], [[Nara Prefecture]] | Latitude Degrees = 34 | Latitude Minutes = 28 | Latitude Seconds = 29.03 | Longitude Degrees = 135 | Longitude Minutes = 47 | Longitude Seconds = 5.52 | ISO = JP-29 | Shape = Unknown | Size = Length: 30 m
Height: 8 m | Burial Facility = Double-winged [[yokoana-style stone chamber]] (Iwayayama style)
(Contains a hewn [[house-shaped sarcophagus]]) | Excavated Items = | Satellite Tombs = | Construction Period = [[7th century]] | Buried Figure = | Mausoleum = | Historic Site = Designated historic site of Nara Prefecture: "Kotani Kofun" | Tangible Cultural Property = | Notes = | Map = Japan Nara | Icon = | Label = Kotani Kofun | Label Position = top }} '''Kotani Kofun''' (小谷古墳) is a [[kofun]] (ancient burial mound) located in {{ill|Kashihara|ja|橿原市}}, [[Nara Prefecture]]. It is designated as a Nara Prefectural Historic Site. == Overview == The Kotani Kofun is situated on the southern slope of a ridge extending northeast from Kaibukiyama at the southern edge of the [[Nara Basin]]Historical Site Explanation Board.. The mound is part of a cluster of eight kofun in the area, positioned at the easternmost point of the group. The mound has suffered significant erosion, and no archaeological excavations have been conducted. The mound's shape is uncertain but is hypothesized to be either circular or square. It measures approximately 30 meters in length and 8 meters in height. The burial facility consists of a {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chambers|ja|横穴式石室}} with double sidewalls, opening to the south. The stone chamber is constructed with well-cut granite blocks and features a sophisticated design similar to the {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} in {{ill|Asuka|ja|明日香村}}, making it an example of the "Iwayayama-style stone chamber." Within the chamber lies a {{ill|house-shaped sarcophagus|ja|家形石棺}} carved from [[Hyogo Prefecture]]'s Tatsuyama Stone (stratified hyaloclastite)Tatsuyama Stone has been identified as rhyolitic stratified hyaloclastite (water-cooled pyroclastic rock) based on recent studies. See [https://web.pref.hyogo.lg.jp/sr09/jibasan/31.html Tatsuyama Stone (Hyogo Prefectural Website)] for details.. The grave goods have not been confirmed due to the lack of excavation. The mound dates to the [[7th century]] during the late [[Kofun period]]. It is recognized as one of the representative terminal period kofun in Nara Prefecture, featuring a stone chamber comparable in scale to those used for royal tombs. While the identity of the interred is unknown, the burial is speculated to belong to an influential aristocratic family, possibly of royal lineage. In the [[Edo period]], it was suggested to be the tomb of [[Empress Saimei]]. The site was designated as a Nara Prefectural Historic Site in [[1975]] (Showa 50). Currently, access to the interior of the stone chamber is restricted. Nearby is the Kotani East Kofun (whose stone chamber remains unopened), and it has been proposed that the two kofun form a twin burial mound pairKunihiko Kawakami, *Asuka Excavation Stories*, Fusosha, 2004, pp. 70-72.. == Burial Facility == [[File:Kotani Kofun, 3D-B-2.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Bird's-eye view of the stone chamber.}}]] [[File:Kotani Kofun, 3D-N-2.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Detailed layout of the stone chamber.}}]] [[File:Kotani Kofun, sekkan 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Layout of the carved sarcophagus.}}]] The burial facility comprises a double sidewall corridor-style stone chamber, opening to the south. Its dimensions are as follows: * '''Total length:''' Approximately 11.6 meters * '''Main chamber:''' Length ~5 meters, Width ~2.8 meters, Height ~2.7 meters * '''Entrance corridor:''' Length ~6.45 meters, Width ~1.9 meters, Height ~1.8 meters The chamber is constructed from large, cut granite blocks with meticulous craftsmanship. Both the rear and side walls of the main chamber consist of two layers, and the seams are sealed with plaster. A single large stone serves as the ceiling. The main chamber floor is elevated one step above the corridor. Inside the main chamber lies a house-shaped sarcophagus carved from Tatsuyama Stone[https://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/kotani.html Kotani Kofun] (Kashihara City "Kashihara Visit Navi").. The lid, almost semicylindrical in shape, lacks suspension lugs, indicating a newer style within house-shaped sarcophagi. The sarcophagus measures 2.4 meters in length, 1.16 meters in width, and 0.82 meters in height. The chamber shares features with the Iwayayama Kofun, classified as "Iwayayama-style." While the main chambers are similar in size, the corridor of Kotani Kofun is shorter. The stone chamber is also nearly identical in design to the {{ill|Minezuka Kofun|ja|峯塚古墳}} in {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}, suggesting a shift from local clan control of stonework to central administration under the imperial court during the terminal kofun periodKunihiko Kawakami, *Asuka Excavation Stories*, Fusosha, 2004, pp. 66-69.. Minezuka Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|Reference: Bird's-eye view of the Minezuka Kofun stone chamber Kotani Kofun, genshitsu-1-2.jpg|Main chamber (view toward the rear wall) Kotani Kofun, sekkan-2.jpg|Carved house-shaped sarcophagus inside the main chamber Kotani Kofun, genshitsu-2-2.jpg|Main chamber (view toward the entrance) Kotani Kofun, sendou-2-2.jpg|Entrance corridor (view toward the entrance) Kotani Kofun, sendou-1-2.jpg|Entrance corridor (view toward the main chamber) Kotani Kofun, entrance-2.jpg|Entrance == Cultural Properties == === Nara Prefectural Cultural Properties === * '''Historic Site''' ** Kotani Kofun: Designated on March 31, 1975 (Showa 50){{PDFlink|[http://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/143530/kenshiitiran.pdf Nara Prefecture Cultural Property List]}} (Nara Prefectural Website).. == References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * Historical site explanation board (installed by the Kashihara City Board of Education). * {{Cite book|year=1981|chapter=Kotani Kofun|title=Nara Prefecture Place names|series=[[Japanese Historical Place name Series]] 30|publisher=[[Heibonsha]]|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|Kotani Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Kotani Kofun}} * [https://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/kotani.html Kotani Kofun] - Kashihara City "Kashihara Visit Navi" In the [[Edo period]], it was suggested to be the tomb of [[Empress Saimei]]. The site was designated as a Nara Prefectural Historic Site in [[1975]] (Showa 50). Currently, access to the interior of the stone chamber is restricted. Nearby is the Kotani East Kofun (whose stone chamber remains unopened), and it has been proposed that the two kofun form a twin burial mound pairKunihiko Kawakami, *Asuka Excavation Stories*, Fusosha, 2004, pp. 70-72.. == Burial Facility == [[File:Kotani Kofun, 3D-B-2.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Bird's-eye view of the stone chamber.}}]] [[File:Kotani Kofun, 3D-N-2.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Detailed layout of the stone chamber.}}]] [[File:Kotani Kofun, sekkan 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Layout of the carved sarcophagus.}}]] The burial facility comprises a double sidewall corridor-style stone chamber, opening to the south. Its dimensions are as follows: * '''Total length:''' Approximately 11.6 meters * '''Main chamber:''' Length ~5 meters, Width ~2.8 meters, Height ~2.7 meters * '''Entrance corridor:''' Length ~6.45 meters, Width ~1.9 meters, Height ~1.8 meters The chamber is constructed from large, cut granite blocks with meticulous craftsmanship. Both the rear and side walls of the main chamber consist of two layers, and the seams are sealed with plaster. A single large stone serves as the ceiling. The main chamber floor is elevated one step above the corridor. Inside the main chamber lies a house-shaped sarcophagus carved from Tatsuyama Stone[https://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/kotani.html Kotani Kofun] (Kashihara City "Kashihara Visit Navi").. The lid, almost semicylindrical in shape, lacks suspension lugs, indicating a newer style within house-shaped sarcophagi. The sarcophagus measures 2.4 meters in length, 1.16 meters in width, and 0.82 meters in height. The chamber shares features with the Iwayayama Kofun, classified as "Iwayayama-style." While the main chambers are similar in size, the corridor of Kotani Kofun is shorter. The stone chamber is also nearly identical in design to the {{ill|Minezuka Kofun|ja|峯塚古墳}} in {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}, suggesting a shift from local clan control of stonework to central administration under the imperial court during the terminal kofun periodKunihiko Kawakami, *Asuka Excavation Stories*, Fusosha, 2004, pp. 66-69.. {{Infobox ancient site | name = Kotani Kofun | image = [[File:Kotani Kofun, entrance.jpg|280px]]
Entrance to the stone chamber | Alternate name = | Affiliation = | location = [[Toriya-cho]], [[Kashihara]], [[Nara Prefecture]] | Latitude Degrees = 34 | Latitude Minutes = 28 | Latitude Seconds = 29.03 | Longitude Degrees = 135 | Longitude Minutes = 47 | Longitude Seconds = 5.52 | ISO = JP-29 | Shape = Unknown | Size = Length: 30 m
Height: 8 m | Burial Facility = Double-winged [[yokoana-style stone chamber]] (Iwayayama style)
(Contains a hewn [[house-shaped sarcophagus]]) | Excavated Items = | Satellite Tombs = | Construction Period = [[7th century]] | Buried Figure = | Mausoleum = | Historic Site = Designated historic site of Nara Prefecture: "Kotani Kofun" | Tangible Cultural Property = | Notes = | Map = Japan Nara | Icon = | Label = Kotani Kofun | Label Position = top }} '''Kotani Kofun''' (小谷古墳) is a [[kofun]] (ancient burial mound) located in {{ill|Kashihara|ja|橿原市}}, [[Nara Prefecture]]. It is designated as a Nara Prefectural Historic Site. == Overview == The Kotani Kofun is situated on the southern slope of a ridge extending northeast from Kaibukiyama at the southern edge of the [[Nara Basin]]Historical Site Explanation Board.. The mound is part of a cluster of eight kofun in the area, positioned at the easternmost point of the group. The mound has suffered significant erosion, and no archaeological excavations have been conducted. The mound's shape is uncertain but is hypothesized to be either circular or square. It measures approximately 30 meters in length and 8 meters in height. The burial facility consists of a {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chambers|ja|横穴式石室}} with double sidewalls, opening to the south. The stone chamber is constructed with well-cut granite blocks and features a sophisticated design similar to the {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} in {{ill|Asuka|ja|明日香村}}, making it an example of the "Iwayayama-style stone chamber." Within the chamber lies a {{ill|house-shaped sarcophagus|ja|家形石棺}} carved from [[Hyogo Prefecture]]'s Tatsuyama Stone (stratified hyaloclastite)Tatsuyama Stone has been identified as rhyolitic stratified hyaloclastite (water-cooled pyroclastic rock) based on recent studies. See [https://web.pref.hyogo.lg.jp/sr09/jibasan/31.html Tatsuyama Stone (Hyogo Prefectural Website)] for details.. The grave goods have not been confirmed due to the lack of excavation. The mound dates to the [[7th century]] during the late [[Kofun period]]. It is recognized as one of the representative terminal period kofun in Nara Prefecture, featuring a stone chamber comparable in scale to those used for royal tombs. While the identity of the interred is unknown, the burial is speculated to belong to an influential aristocratic family, possibly of royal lineage. In the [[Edo period]], it was suggested to be the tomb of [[Empress Saimei]]. The site was designated as a Nara Prefectural Historic Site in [[1975]] (Showa 50). Currently, access to the interior of the stone chamber is restricted. Nearby is the Kotani East Kofun (whose stone chamber remains unopened), and it has been proposed that the two kofun form a twin burial mound pairKunihiko Kawakami, *Asuka Excavation Stories*, Fusosha, 2004, pp. 70-72.. == Burial Facility == [[File:Kotani Kofun, 3D-B-2.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Bird's-eye view of the stone chamber.}}]] [[File:Kotani Kofun, 3D-N-2.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Detailed layout of the stone chamber.}}]] [[File:Kotani Kofun, sekkan 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Layout of the carved sarcophagus.}}]] The burial facility comprises a double sidewall corridor-style stone chamber, opening to the south. Its dimensions are as follows: * '''Total length:''' Approximately 11.6 meters * '''Main chamber:''' Length ~5 meters, Width ~2.8 meters, Height ~2.7 meters * '''Entrance corridor:''' Length ~6.45 meters, Width ~1.9 meters, Height ~1.8 meters The chamber is constructed from large, cut granite blocks with meticulous craftsmanship. Both the rear and side walls of the main chamber consist of two layers, and the seams are sealed with plaster. A single large stone serves as the ceiling. The main chamber floor is elevated one step above the corridor. Inside the main chamber lies a house-shaped sarcophagus carved from Tatsuyama Stone[https://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/kotani.html Kotani Kofun] (Kashihara City "Kashihara Visit Navi").. The lid, almost semicylindrical in shape, lacks suspension lugs, indicating a newer style within house-shaped sarcophagi. The sarcophagus measures 2.4 meters in length, 1.16 meters in width, and 0.82 meters in height. The chamber shares features with the Iwayayama Kofun, classified as "Iwayayama-style." While the main chambers are similar in size, the corridor of Kotani Kofun is shorter. The stone chamber is also nearly identical in design to the {{ill|Minezuka Kofun|ja|峯塚古墳}} in {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}, suggesting a shift from local clan control of stonework to central administration under the imperial court during the terminal kofun periodKunihiko Kawakami, *Asuka Excavation Stories*, Fusosha, 2004, pp. 66-69.. It is designated as a Nara Prefectural Historic Site. == Overview == The Kotani Kofun is situated on the southern slope of a ridge extending northeast from Kaibukiyama at the southern edge of the [[Nara Basin]]Historical Site Explanation Board.. The mound is part of a cluster of eight kofun in the area, positioned at the easternmost point of the group. The mound has suffered significant erosion, and no archaeological excavations have been conducted. The mound's shape is uncertain but is hypothesized to be either circular or square. It measures approximately 30 meters in length and 8 meters in height. The burial facility consists of a {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chambers|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} with double sidewalls, opening to the south. The stone chamber is constructed with well-cut granite blocks and features a sophisticated design similar to the {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} in {{ill|Asuka|ja|明日香村}}, making it an example of the "Iwayayama-style stone chamber." Within the chamber lies a {{ill|house-shaped sarcophagus|ja|家形石棺}} carved from [[Hyogo Prefecture]]'s Tatsuyama Stone (stratified hyaloclastite)Tatsuyama Stone has been identified as rhyolitic stratified hyaloclastite (water-cooled pyroclastic rock) based on recent studies. See [https://web.pref.hyogo.lg.jp/sr09/jibasan/31.html Tatsuyama Stone (Hyogo Prefectural Website)] for details.. The grave goods have not been confirmed due to the lack of excavation. The mound dates to the [[7th century]] during the late [[Kofun period]]. It is recognized as one of the representative terminal period kofun in Nara Prefecture, featuring a stone chamber comparable in scale to those used for royal tombs. While the identity of the interred is unknown, the burial is speculated to belong to an influential aristocratic family, possibly of royal lineage. In the [[Edo period]], it was suggested to be the tomb of [[Empress Saimei]]. The site was designated as a Nara Prefectural Historic Site in [[1975]] (Showa 50). Currently, access to the interior of the stone chamber is restricted. Nearby is the Kotani East Kofun (whose stone chamber remains unopened), and it has been proposed that the two kofun form a twin burial mound pairKunihiko Kawakami, *Asuka Excavation Stories*, Fusosha, 2004, pp. 70-72.. == Burial Facility == [[File:Kotani Kofun, 3D-B-2.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Bird's-eye view of the stone chamber.}}]] [[File:Kotani Kofun, 3D-N-2.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Detailed layout of the stone chamber.}}]] [[File:Kotani Kofun, sekkan 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Layout of the carved sarcophagus.}}]] The burial facility comprises a double sidewall corridor-style stone chamber, opening to the south. Its dimensions are as follows: * '''Total length:''' Approximately 11.6 meters * '''Main chamber:''' Length ~5 meters, Width ~2.8 meters, Height ~2.7 meters * '''Entrance corridor:''' Length ~6.45 meters, Width ~1.9 meters, Height ~1.8 meters The chamber is constructed from large, cut granite blocks with meticulous craftsmanship. Both the rear and side walls of the main chamber consist of two layers, and the seams are sealed with plaster. A single large stone serves as the ceiling. The main chamber floor is elevated one step above the corridor. Inside the main chamber lies a house-shaped sarcophagus carved from Tatsuyama Stone[https://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/kotani.html Kotani Kofun] (Kashihara City "Kashihara Visit Navi").. The lid, almost semicylindrical in shape, lacks suspension lugs, indicating a newer style within house-shaped sarcophagi. The sarcophagus measures 2.4 meters in length, 1.16 meters in width, and 0.82 meters in height. The chamber shares features with the Iwayayama Kofun, classified as "Iwayayama-style." While the main chambers are similar in size, the corridor of Kotani Kofun is shorter. The stone chamber is also nearly identical in design to the {{ill|Minezuka Kofun|ja|峯塚古墳}} in {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}, suggesting a shift from local clan control of stonework to central administration under the imperial court during the terminal kofun periodKunihiko Kawakami, *Asuka Excavation Stories*, Fusosha, 2004, pp. 66-69.. Minezuka Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|Reference: Bird's-eye view of the Minezuka Kofun stone chamber Kotani Kofun, genshitsu-1-2.jpg|Main chamber (view toward the rear wall) Kotani Kofun, sekkan-2.jpg|Carved house-shaped sarcophagus inside the main chamber Kotani Kofun, genshitsu-2-2.jpg|Main chamber (view toward the entrance) Kotani Kofun, sendou-2-2.jpg|Entrance corridor (view toward the entrance) Kotani Kofun, sendou-1-2.jpg|Entrance corridor (view toward the main chamber) Kotani Kofun, entrance-2.jpg|Entrance == Cultural Properties == === Nara Prefectural Cultural Properties === * '''Historic Site''' ** Kotani Kofun: Designated on March 31, 1975 (Showa 50){{PDFlink|[http://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/143530/kenshiitiran.pdf Nara Prefecture Cultural Property List]}} (Nara Prefectural Website).. == References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * Historical site explanation board (installed by the Kashihara City Board of Education). * {{Cite book|year=1981|chapter=Kotani Kofun|title=Nara Prefecture Place names|series=[[Japanese Historical Place name Series]] 30|publisher=[[Heibonsha]]|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|Kotani Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}}} == External Links == {{Infobox ancient site | name = Kotani Kofun | image = [[File:Kotani Kofun, entrance.jpg|280px]]
Entrance to the stone chamber | Alternate name = | Affiliation = | location = [[Toriya-cho]], [[Kashihara]], [[Nara Prefecture]] | Latitude Degrees = 34 | Latitude Minutes = 28 | Latitude Seconds = 29.03 | Longitude Degrees = 135 | Longitude Minutes = 47 | Longitude Seconds = 5.52 | ISO = JP-29 | Shape = Unknown | Size = Length: 30 m
Height: 8 m | Burial Facility = Double-winged [[yokoana-style stone chamber]] (Iwayayama style)
(Contains a hewn [[house-shaped sarcophagus]]) | Excavated Items = | Satellite Tombs = | Construction Period = [[7th century]] | Buried Figure = | Mausoleum = | Historic Site = Designated historic site of Nara Prefecture: "Kotani Kofun" | Tangible Cultural Property = | Notes = | Map = Japan Nara | Icon = | Label = Kotani Kofun | Label Position = top }} '''Kotani Kofun''' (小谷古墳) is a [[kofun]] (ancient burial mound) located in {{ill|Kashihara|ja|橿原市}}, [[Nara Prefecture]]. It is designated as a Nara Prefectural Historic Site. == Overview == The Kotani Kofun is situated on the southern slope of a ridge extending northeast from Kaibukiyama at the southern edge of the [[Nara Basin]]Historical Site Explanation Board.. The mound is part of a cluster of eight kofun in the area, positioned at the easternmost point of the group. The mound has suffered significant erosion, and no archaeological excavations have been conducted. The mound's shape is uncertain but is hypothesized to be either circular or square. It measures approximately 30 meters in length and 8 meters in height. The burial facility consists of a {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chambers|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} with double sidewalls, opening to the south. The stone chamber is constructed with well-cut granite blocks and features a sophisticated design similar to the {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} in {{ill|Asuka|ja|明日香村}}, making it an example of the "Iwayayama-style stone chamber." Within the chamber lies a {{ill|house-shaped sarcophagus|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus}} carved from [[Hyogo Prefecture]]'s Tatsuyama Stone (stratified hyaloclastite)Tatsuyama Stone has been identified as rhyolitic stratified hyaloclastite (water-cooled pyroclastic rock) based on recent studies. See [https://web.pref.hyogo.lg.jp/sr09/jibasan/31.html Tatsuyama Stone (Hyogo Prefectural Website)] for details.. The grave goods have not been confirmed due to the lack of excavation. The mound dates to the [[7th century]] during the late [[Kofun period]]. It is recognized as one of the representative terminal period kofun in Nara Prefecture, featuring a stone chamber comparable in scale to those used for royal tombs. While the identity of the interred is unknown, the burial is speculated to belong to an influential aristocratic family, possibly of royal lineage. In the [[Edo period]], it was suggested to be the tomb of [[Empress Saimei]]. The site was designated as a Nara Prefectural Historic Site in [[1975]] (Showa 50). Currently, access to the interior of the stone chamber is restricted. Nearby is the Kotani East Kofun (whose stone chamber remains unopened), and it has been proposed that the two kofun form a twin burial mound pairKunihiko Kawakami, *Asuka Excavation Stories*, Fusosha, 2004, pp. 70-72.. == Burial Facility == [[File:Kotani Kofun, 3D-B-2.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Bird's-eye view of the stone chamber.}}]] [[File:Kotani Kofun, 3D-N-2.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Detailed layout of the stone chamber.}}]] [[File:Kotani Kofun, sekkan 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Layout of the carved sarcophagus.}}]] The burial facility comprises a double sidewall corridor-style stone chamber, opening to the south. Its dimensions are as follows: * '''Total length:''' Approximately 11.6 meters * '''Main chamber:''' Length ~5 meters, Width ~2.8 meters, Height ~2.7 meters * '''Entrance corridor:''' Length ~6.45 meters, Width ~1.9 meters, Height ~1.8 meters The chamber is constructed from large, cut granite blocks with meticulous craftsmanship. Both the rear and side walls of the main chamber consist of two layers, and the seams are sealed with plaster. A single large stone serves as the ceiling. The main chamber floor is elevated one step above the corridor. Inside the main chamber lies a house-shaped sarcophagus carved from Tatsuyama Stone[https://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/kotani.html Kotani Kofun] (Kashihara City "Kashihara Visit Navi").. The lid, almost semicylindrical in shape, lacks suspension lugs, indicating a newer style within house-shaped sarcophagi. The sarcophagus measures 2.4 meters in length, 1.16 meters in width, and 0.82 meters in height. The chamber shares features with the Iwayayama Kofun, classified as "Iwayayama-style." While the main chambers are similar in size, the corridor of Kotani Kofun is shorter. The stone chamber is also nearly identical in design to the {{ill|Minezuka Kofun|ja|峯塚古墳}} in {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}, suggesting a shift from local clan control of stonework to central administration under the imperial court during the terminal kofun periodKunihiko Kawakami, *Asuka Excavation Stories*, Fusosha, 2004, pp. 66-69.. Minezuka Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|Reference: Bird's-eye view of the Minezuka Kofun stone chamber Kotani Kofun, genshitsu-1-2.jpg|Main chamber (view toward the rear wall) Kotani Kofun, sekkan-2.jpg|Carved house-shaped sarcophagus inside the main chamber Kotani Kofun, genshitsu-2-2.jpg|Main chamber (view toward the entrance) Kotani Kofun, sendou-2-2.jpg|Entrance corridor (view toward the entrance) Kotani Kofun, sendou-1-2.jpg|Entrance corridor (view toward the main chamber) Kotani Kofun, entrance-2.jpg|Entrance == Cultural Properties == === Nara Prefectural Cultural Properties === * '''Historic Site''' ** Kotani Kofun: Designated on March 31, 1975 (Showa 50){{PDFlink|[http://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/143530/kenshiitiran.pdf Nara Prefecture Cultural Property List]}} (Nara Prefectural Website).. revision.text:
{{Infobox ancient site | name = Kotani Kofun | image = [[File:Kotani Kofun, entrance.jpg|280px]]
Entrance to the stone chamber | Alternate name = | Affiliation = | location = [[Toriya-cho]], [[Kashihara]], [[Nara Prefecture]] | Latitude Degrees = 34 | Latitude Minutes = 28 | Latitude Seconds = 29.03 | Longitude Degrees = 135 | Longitude Minutes = 47 | Longitude Seconds = 5.52 | ISO = JP-29 | Shape = Unknown | Size = Length: 30 m
Height: 8 m | Burial Facility = Double-winged [[yokoana-style stone chamber]] (Iwayayama style)
(Contains a hewn [[house-shaped sarcophagus]]) | Excavated Items = | Satellite Tombs = | Construction Period = [[7th century]] | Buried Figure = | Mausoleum = | Historic Site = Designated historic site of Nara Prefecture: "Kotani Kofun" | Tangible Cultural Property = | Notes = | Map = Japan Nara | Icon = | Label = Kotani Kofun | Label Position = top }} '''Kotani Kofun''' (小谷古墳) is a [[kofun]] (ancient burial mound) located in {{ill|Kashihara|ja|橿原市}}, [[Nara Prefecture]]. It is designated as a Nara Prefectural Historic Site. == Overview == The Kotani Kofun is situated on the southern slope of a ridge extending northeast from Kaibukiyama at the southern edge of the [[Nara Basin]]Historical Site Explanation Board.. The mound is part of a cluster of eight kofun in the area, positioned at the easternmost point of the group. The mound has suffered significant erosion, and no archaeological excavations have been conducted. The mound's shape is uncertain but is hypothesized to be either circular or square. It measures approximately 30 meters in length and 8 meters in height. The burial facility consists of a {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chambers|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} with double sidewalls, opening to the south. The stone chamber is constructed with well-cut granite blocks and features a sophisticated design similar to the {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} in {{ill|Asuka|ja|明日香村}}, making it an example of the "Iwayayama-style stone chamber." Within the chamber lies a {{ill|house-shaped sarcophagus|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus}} carved from [[Hyogo Prefecture]]'s Tatsuyama Stone (stratified hyaloclastite)Tatsuyama Stone has been identified as rhyolitic stratified hyaloclastite (water-cooled pyroclastic rock) based on recent studies. See [https://web.pref.hyogo.lg.jp/sr09/jibasan/31.html Tatsuyama Stone (Hyogo Prefectural Website)] for details.. The grave goods have not been confirmed due to the lack of excavation. The mound dates to the [[7th century]] during the late [[Kofun period]]. It is recognized as one of the representative terminal period kofun in Nara Prefecture, featuring a stone chamber comparable in scale to those used for royal tombs. While the identity of the interred is unknown, the burial is speculated to belong to an influential aristocratic family, possibly of royal lineage. In the [[Edo period]], it was suggested to be the tomb of [[Empress Saimei]]. The site was designated as a Nara Prefectural Historic Site in [[1975]] (Showa 50). Currently, access to the interior of the stone chamber is restricted. Nearby is the Kotani East Kofun (whose stone chamber remains unopened), and it has been proposed that the two kofun form a twin burial mound pairKunihiko Kawakami, *Asuka Excavation Stories*, Fusosha, 2004, pp. 70-72.. == Burial Facility == [[File:Kotani Kofun, 3D-B-2.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Bird's-eye view of the stone chamber.}}]] [[File:Kotani Kofun, 3D-N-2.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Detailed layout of the stone chamber.}}]] [[File:Kotani Kofun, sekkan 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Layout of the carved sarcophagus.}}]] The burial facility comprises a double sidewall corridor-style stone chamber, opening to the south. Its dimensions are as follows: * '''Total length:''' Approximately 11.6 meters * '''Main chamber:''' Length ~5 meters, Width ~2.8 meters, Height ~2.7 meters * '''Entrance corridor:''' Length ~6.45 meters, Width ~1.9 meters, Height ~1.8 meters The chamber is constructed from large, cut granite blocks with meticulous craftsmanship. Both the rear and side walls of the main chamber consist of two layers, and the seams are sealed with plaster. A single large stone serves as the ceiling. The main chamber floor is elevated one step above the corridor. Inside the main chamber lies a house-shaped sarcophagus carved from Tatsuyama Stone[https://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/kotani.html Kotani Kofun] (Kashihara City "Kashihara Visit Navi").. The lid, almost semicylindrical in shape, lacks suspension lugs, indicating a newer style within house-shaped sarcophagi. The sarcophagus measures 2.4 meters in length, 1.16 meters in width, and 0.82 meters in height. The chamber shares features with the Iwayayama Kofun, classified as "Iwayayama-style." While the main chambers are similar in size, the corridor of Kotani Kofun is shorter. The stone chamber is also nearly identical in design to the {{ill|Minezuka Kofun|ja|峯塚古墳}} in {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}, suggesting a shift from local clan control of stonework to central administration under the imperial court during the terminal kofun periodKunihiko Kawakami, *Asuka Excavation Stories*, Fusosha, 2004, pp. 66-69.. Minezuka Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|Reference: Bird's-eye view of the Minezuka Kofun stone chamber Kotani Kofun, genshitsu-1-2.jpg|Main chamber (view toward the rear wall) Kotani Kofun, sekkan-2.jpg|Carved house-shaped sarcophagus inside the main chamber Kotani Kofun, genshitsu-2-2.jpg|Main chamber (view toward the entrance) Kotani Kofun, sendou-2-2.jpg|Entrance corridor (view toward the entrance) Kotani Kofun, sendou-1-2.jpg|Entrance corridor (view toward the main chamber) Kotani Kofun, entrance-2.jpg|Entrance == Cultural Properties == === Nara Prefectural Cultural Properties === * '''Historic Site''' ** Kotani Kofun: Designated on March 31, 1975 (Showa 50){{PDFlink|[http://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/143530/kenshiitiran.pdf Nara Prefecture Cultural Property List]}} (Nara Prefectural Website).. == References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * Historical site explanation board (installed by the Kashihara City Board of Education). * {{Cite book|year=1981|chapter=Kotani Kofun|title=Nara Prefecture Place names|series=[[Japanese Historical Place name Series]] 30|publisher=[[Heibonsha]]|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|Kotani Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Kotani Kofun}} * [https://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/kotani.html Kotani Kofun] - Kashihara City "Kashihara Visit Navi" The lid, almost semicylindrical in shape, lacks suspension lugs, indicating a newer style within house-shaped sarcophagi. The sarcophagus measures 2.4 meters in length, 1.16 meters in width, and 0.82 meters in height. The chamber shares features with the Iwayayama Kofun, classified as "Iwayayama-style." While the main chambers are similar in size, the corridor of Kotani Kofun is shorter. The stone chamber is also nearly identical in design to the {{ill|Minezuka Kofun|ja|峯塚古墳}} in {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}, suggesting a shift from local clan control of stonework to central administration under the imperial court during the terminal kofun periodKunihiko Kawakami, *Asuka Excavation Stories*, Fusosha, 2004, pp. 66-69.. {{Infobox ancient site | name = Kotani Kofun | image = [[File:Kotani Kofun, entrance.jpg|280px]]
Entrance to the stone chamber | Alternate name = | Affiliation = | location = [[Toriya-cho]], [[Kashihara]], [[Nara Prefecture]] | Latitude Degrees = 34 | Latitude Minutes = 28 | Latitude Seconds = 29.03 | Longitude Degrees = 135 | Longitude Minutes = 47 | Longitude Seconds = 5.52 | ISO = JP-29 | Shape = Unknown | Size = Length: 30 m
Height: 8 m | Burial Facility = Double-winged [[yokoana-style stone chamber]] (Iwayayama style)
(Contains a hewn [[house-shaped sarcophagus]]) | Excavated Items = | Satellite Tombs = | Construction Period = [[7th century]] | Buried Figure = | Mausoleum = | Historic Site = Designated historic site of Nara Prefecture: "Kotani Kofun" | Tangible Cultural Property = | Notes = | Map = Japan Nara | Icon = | Label = Kotani Kofun | Label Position = top }} '''Kotani Kofun''' (小谷古墳) is a [[kofun]] (ancient burial mound) located in {{ill|Kashihara|ja|橿原市}}, [[Nara Prefecture]]. It is designated as a Nara Prefectural Historic Site. == Overview == The Kotani Kofun is situated on the southern slope of a ridge extending northeast from Kaibukiyama at the southern edge of the [[Nara Basin]]Historical Site Explanation Board.. The mound is part of a cluster of eight kofun in the area, positioned at the easternmost point of the group. The mound has suffered significant erosion, and no archaeological excavations have been conducted. The mound's shape is uncertain but is hypothesized to be either circular or square. It measures approximately 30 meters in length and 8 meters in height. The burial facility consists of a {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chambers|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} with double sidewalls, opening to the south. The stone chamber is constructed with well-cut granite blocks and features a sophisticated design similar to the {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} in {{ill|Asuka|ja|明日香村}}, making it an example of the "Iwayayama-style stone chamber." Within the chamber lies a {{ill|house-shaped sarcophagus|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus}} carved from [[Hyogo Prefecture]]'s Tatsuyama Stone (stratified hyaloclastite)Tatsuyama Stone has been identified as rhyolitic stratified hyaloclastite (water-cooled pyroclastic rock) based on recent studies. See [https://web.pref.hyogo.lg.jp/sr09/jibasan/31.html Tatsuyama Stone (Hyogo Prefectural Website)] for details.. The grave goods have not been confirmed due to the lack of excavation. The mound dates to the [[7th century]] during the late [[Kofun period]]. It is recognized as one of the representative terminal period kofun in Nara Prefecture, featuring a stone chamber comparable in scale to those used for royal tombs. While the identity of the interred is unknown, the burial is speculated to belong to an influential aristocratic family, possibly of royal lineage. In the [[Edo period]], it was suggested to be the tomb of [[Empress Saimei]]. The site was designated as a Nara Prefectural Historic Site in [[1975]] (Showa 50). Currently, access to the interior of the stone chamber is restricted. Nearby is the Kotani East Kofun (whose stone chamber remains unopened), and it has been proposed that the two kofun form a twin burial mound pairKunihiko Kawakami, *Asuka Excavation Stories*, Fusosha, 2004, pp. 70-72.. == Burial Facility == [[File:Kotani Kofun, 3D-B-2.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Bird's-eye view of the stone chamber.}}]] [[File:Kotani Kofun, 3D-N-2.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Detailed layout of the stone chamber.}}]] [[File:Kotani Kofun, sekkan 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Layout of the carved sarcophagus.}}]] The burial facility comprises a double sidewall corridor-style stone chamber, opening to the south. Its dimensions are as follows: * '''Total length:''' Approximately qq.6 meters * '''Main chamber:''' Length ~5 meters, Width ~2.8 meters, Height ~2.7 meters * '''Entrance corridor:''' Length ~6.45 meters, Width ~1.9 meters, Height ~1.8 meters The chamber is constructed from large, cut granite blocks with meticulous craftsmanship. Both the rear and side walls of the main chamber consist of two layers, and the seams are sealed with plaster. A single large stone serves as the ceiling. The main chamber floor is elevated one step above the corridor. Inside the main chamber lies a house-shaped sarcophagus carved from Tatsuyama Stone[https://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/kotani.html Kotani Kofun] (Kashihara City "Kashihara Visit Navi").. The lid, almost semicylindrical in shape, lacks suspension lugs, indicating a newer style within house-shaped sarcophagi. The sarcophagus measures 2.4 meters in length, 1.16 meters in width, and 0.82 meters in height. The chamber shares features with the Iwayayama Kofun, classified as "Iwayayama-style." While the main chambers are similar in size, the corridor of Kotani Kofun is shorter. The stone chamber is also nearly identical in design to the {{ill|Minezuka Kofun|ja|峯塚古墳}} in {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}, suggesting a shift from local clan control of stonework to central administration under the imperial court during the terminal kofun periodKunihiko Kawakami, *Asuka Excavation Stories*, Fusosha, 2004, pp. 66-69.. The stone chamber is constructed with well-cut granite blocks and features a sophisticated design similar to the {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} in {{ill|Asuka|ja|明日香村}}, making it an example of the "Iwayayama-style stone chamber." Within the chamber lies a {{ill|house-shaped sarcophagus|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus}} carved from [[Hyogo Prefecture]]'s Tatsuyama Stone (stratified hyaloclastite)Tatsuyama Stone has been identified as rhyolitic stratified hyaloclastite (water-cooled pyroclastic rock) based on recent studies. See [https://web.pref.hyogo.lg.jp/sr09/jibasan/31.html Tatsuyama Stone (Hyogo Prefectural Website)] for details.. The grave goods have not been confirmed due to the lack of excavation. The mound dates to the [[7th century]] during the late [[Kofun period]]. It is recognized as one of the representative terminal period kofun in Nara Prefecture, featuring a stone chamber comparable in scale to those used for royal tombs. While the identity of the interred is unknown, the burial is speculated to belong to an influential aristocratic family, possibly of royal lineage. In the [[Edo period]], it was suggested to be the tomb of [[Empress Saimei]]. The site was designated as a Nara Prefectural Historic Site in [[1975]] (Showa 50). Currently, access to the interior of the stone chamber is restricted. Nearby is the Kotani East Kofun (whose stone chamber remains unopened), and it has been proposed that the two kofun form a twin burial mound pairKunihiko Kawakami, *Asuka Excavation Stories*, Fusosha, 2004, pp. 70-72.. == Burial Facility == [[File:Kotani Kofun, 3D-B-2.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Bird's-eye view of the stone chamber.}}]] [[File:Kotani Kofun, 3D-N-2.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Detailed layout of the stone chamber.}}]] [[File:Kotani Kofun, sekkan 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Layout of the carved sarcophagus.}}]] The burial facility comprises a double sidewall corridor-style stone chamber, opening to the south. Its dimensions are as follows: * '''Total length:''' Approximately qq.6 meters * '''Main chamber:''' Length ~5 meters, Width ~2.8 meters, Height ~2.7 meters * '''Entrance corridor:''' Length ~6.45 meters, Width ~1.9 meters, Height ~1.8 meters The chamber is constructed from large, cut granite blocks with meticulous craftsmanship. Both the rear and side walls of the main chamber consist of two layers, and the seams are sealed with plaster. A single large stone serves as the ceiling. The main chamber floor is elevated one step above the corridor. Inside the main chamber lies a house-shaped sarcophagus carved from Tatsuyama Stone[https://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/kotani.html Kotani Kofun] (Kashihara City "Kashihara Visit Navi").. The lid, almost semicylindrical in shape, lacks suspension lugs, indicating a newer style within house-shaped sarcophagi. The sarcophagus measures 2.4 meters in length, 1.16 meters in width, and 0.82 meters in height. The chamber shares features with the Iwayayama Kofun, classified as "Iwayayama-style." While the main chambers are similar in size, the corridor of Kotani Kofun is shorter. The stone chamber is also nearly identical in design to the {{ill|Minezuka Kofun|ja|峯塚古墳}} in {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}, suggesting a shift from local clan control of stonework to central administration under the imperial court during the terminal kofun periodKunihiko Kawakami, *Asuka Excavation Stories*, Fusosha, 2004, pp. 66-69.. Minezuka Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|Reference: Bird's-eye view of the Minezuka Kofun stone chamber Kotani Kofun, genshitsu-1-2.jpg|Main chamber (view toward the rear wall) Kotani Kofun, sekkan-2.jpg|Carved house-shaped sarcophagus inside the main chamber Kotani Kofun, genshitsu-2-2.jpg|Main chamber (view toward the entrance) Kotani Kofun, sendou-2-2.jpg|Entrance corridor (view toward the entrance) Kotani Kofun, sendou-1-2.jpg|Entrance corridor (view toward the main chamber) Kotani Kofun, entrance-2.jpg|Entrance == Cultural Properties == === Nara Prefectural Cultural Properties === * '''Historic Site''' ** Kotani Kofun: Designated on March 31, 1975 (Showa 50){{PDFlink|[http://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/143530/kenshiitiran.pdf Nara Prefecture Cultural Property List]}} (Nara Prefectural Website).. (Contains a hewn [[house-shaped sarcophagus]]) | Excavated Items = | Satellite Tombs = | Construction Period = [[7th century]] | Buried Figure = | Mausoleum = | Historic Site = Designated historic site of Nara Prefecture: "Kotani Kofun" | Tangible Cultural Property = | Notes = | Map = Japan Nara | Icon = | Label = Kotani Kofun | Label Position = top }} '''Kotani Kofun''' (小谷古墳) is a [[kofun]] (ancient burial mound) located in {{ill|Kashihara|ja|橿原市}}, [[Nara Prefecture]]. It is designated as a Nara Prefectural Historic Site. == Overview == The Kotani Kofun is situated on the southern slope of a ridge extending northeast from Kaibukiyama at the southern edge of the [[Nara Basin]]Historical Site Explanation Board.. The mound is part of a cluster of eight kofun in the area, positioned at the easternmost point of the group. The mound has suffered significant erosion, and no archaeological excavations have been conducted. The mound's shape is uncertain but is hypothesized to be either circular or square. It measures aqqroximately 30 meters in length and 8 meters in height. The burial facility consists of a {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chambers|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} with double sidewalls, opening to the south. The stone chamber is constructed with well-cut granite blocks and features a sophisticated design similar to the {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} in {{ill|Asuka|ja|明日香村}}, making it an example of the "Iwayayama-style stone chamber." While the identity of the interred is unknown, the burial is speculated to belong to an influential aristocratic family, possibly of royal lineage. In the [[Edo period]], it was suggested to be the tomb of [[Empress Saimei]]. The site was designated as a Nara Prefectural Historic Site in [[1975]] (Showa 50). Currently, access to the interior of the stone chamber is restricted. Nearby is the Kotani East Kofun (whose stone chamber remains unopened), and it has been proposed that the two kofun form a twin burial mound pairKunihiko Kawakami, *Asuka Excavation Stories*, Fusosha, 2004, qq. 70-72.. == Burial Facility == [[File:Kotani Kofun, 3D-B-2.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Bird's-eye view of the stone chamber.}}]] [[File:Kotani Kofun, 3D-N-2.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Detailed layout of the stone chamber.}}]] [[File:Kotani Kofun, sekkan 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Layout of the carved sarcophagus.}}]] The burial facility comprises a double sidewall corridor-style stone chamber, opening to the south. The lid, almost semicylindrical in shape, lacks suspension lugs, indicating a newer style within house-shaped sarcophagi. The sarcophagus measures 2.4 meters in length, 1.16 meters in width, and 0.82 meters in height. The chamber shares features with the Iwayayama Kofun, classified as "Iwayayama-style." While the main chambers are similar in size, the corridor of Kotani Kofun is shorter. {{Infobox ancient site | name = Kotani Kofun | image = [[File:Kotani Kofun, entrance.jpg|280px]]
Entrance to the stone chamber | Alternate name = | Affiliation = | location = [[Toriya-cho]], [[Kashihara]], [[Nara Prefecture]] | Latitude Degrees = 34 | Latitude Minutes = 28 | Latitude Seconds = 29.03 | Longitude Degrees = 135 | Longitude Minutes = 47 | Longitude Seconds = 5.52 | ISO = JP-29 | Shape = Unknown | Size = Length: 30 m
Height: 8 m | Burial Facility = Double-winged [[yokoana-style stone chamber]] (Iwayayama style)
(Contains a hewn [[house-shaped sarcophagus]]) | Excavated Items = | Satellite Tombs = | Construction Period = [[7th century]] | Buried Figure = | Mausoleum = | Historic Site = Designated historic site of Nara Prefecture: "Kotani Kofun" | Tangible Cultural Property = | Notes = | Map = Japan Nara | Icon = | Label = Kotani Kofun | Label Position = top }} '''Kotani Kofun''' (小谷古墳) is a [[kofun]] (ancient burial mound) located in {{ill|Kashihara|ja|橿原市}}, [[Nara Prefecture]]. It is designated as a Nara Prefectural Historic Site. == Overview == The Kotani Kofun is situated on the southern slope of a ridge extending northeast from Kaibukiyama at the southern edge of the [[Nara Basin]]Historical Site Explanation Board.. The mound is part of a cluster of eight kofun in the area, positioned at the easternmost point of the group. The mound has suffered significant erosion, and no archaeological excavations have been conducted. The mound's shape is uncertain but is hypothesized to be either circular or square. It measures aqqroximately 30 meters in length and 8 meters in height. The burial facility consists of a {{ill|Yokoana-style stone chambers|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} with double sidewalls, opening to the south. The stone chamber is constructed with well-cut granite blocks and features a sophisticated design similar to the {{ill|Iwayayama Kofun|ja|岩屋山古墳}} in {{ill|Asuka|ja|明日香村}}, making it an example of the "Iwayayama-style stone chamber." Within the chamber lies a {{ill|house-shaped sarcophagus|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus}} carved from [[Hyogo Prefecture]]'s Tatsuyama Stone (stratified hyaloclastite)Tatsuyama Stone has been identified as rhyolitic stratified hyaloclastite (water-cooled pyroclastic rock) based on recent studies. See [https://web.pref.hyogo.lg.jp/sr09/jibasan/31.html Tatsuyama Stone (Hyogo Prefectural Website)] for details.. The grave goods have not been confirmed due to the lack of excavation. The mound dates to the [[7th century]] during the late [[Kofun period]]. It is recognized as one of the representative terminal period kofun in Nara Prefecture, featuring a stone chamber comparable in scale to those used for royal tombs. While the identity of the interred is unknown, the burial is speculated to belong to an influential aristocratic family, possibly of royal lineage. In the [[Edo period]], it was suggested to be the tomb of [[Empress Saimei]]. The site was designated as a Nara Prefectural Historic Site in [[1975]] (Showa 50). Currently, access to the interior of the stone chamber is restricted. Nearby is the Kotani East Kofun (whose stone chamber remains unopened), and it has been proposed that the two kofun form a twin burial mound pairKunihiko Kawakami, *Asuka Excavation Stories*, Fusosha, 2004, qq. 70-72.. == Burial Facility == [[File:Kotani Kofun, 3D-B-2.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Bird's-eye view of the stone chamber.}}]] [[File:Kotani Kofun, 3D-N-2.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Detailed layout of the stone chamber.}}]] [[File:Kotani Kofun, sekkan 3D-N.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Layout of the carved sarcophagus.}}]] The burial facility comprises a double sidewall corridor-style stone chamber, opening to the south. Its dimensions are as follows: * '''Total length:''' Aqqroximately qq.6 meters * '''Main chamber:''' Length ~5 meters, Width ~2.8 meters, Height ~2.7 meters * '''Entrance corridor:''' Length ~6.45 meters, Width ~1.9 meters, Height ~1.8 meters The chamber is constructed from large, cut granite blocks with meticulous craftsmanship. Both the rear and side walls of the main chamber consist of two layers, and the seams are sealed with plaster. A single large stone serves as the ceiling. The main chamber floor is elevated one step above the corridor. Inside the main chamber lies a house-shaped sarcophagus carved from Tatsuyama Stone[https://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/kotani.html Kotani Kofun] (Kashihara City "Kashihara Visit Navi").. The lid, almost semicylindrical in shape, lacks suspension lugs, indicating a newer style within house-shaped sarcophagi. The sarcophagus measures 2.4 meters in length, 1.16 meters in width, and 0.82 meters in height. The chamber shares features with the Iwayayama Kofun, classified as "Iwayayama-style." While the main chambers are similar in size, the corridor of Kotani Kofun is shorter. The stone chamber is also nearly identical in design to the {{ill|Minezuka Kofun|ja|峯塚古墳}} in {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}, suggesting a shift from local clan control of stonework to central administration under the imperial court during the terminal kofun periodKunihiko Kawakami, *Asuka Excavation Stories*, Fusosha, 2004, qq. 66-69.. Minezuka Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|Reference: Bird's-eye view of the Minezuka Kofun stone chamber Kotani Kofun, genshitsu-1-2.jpg|Main chamber (view toward the rear wall) Kotani Kofun, sekkan-2.jpg|Carved house-shaped sarcophagus inside the main chamber Kotani Kofun, genshitsu-2-2.jpg|Main chamber (view toward the entrance) Kotani Kofun, sendou-2-2.jpg|Entrance corridor (view toward the entrance) Kotani Kofun, sendou-1-2.jpg|Entrance corridor (view toward the main chamber) Kotani Kofun, entrance-2.jpg|Entrance == Cultural Properties == === Nara Prefectural Cultural Properties === * '''Historic Site''' ** Kotani Kofun: Designated on March 31, 1975 (Showa 50){{PDFlink|[http://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/143530/kenshiitiran.pdf Nara Prefecture Cultural Property List]}} (Nara Prefectural Website).. == References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * Historical site explanation board (installed by the Kashihara City Board of Education). * {{Cite book|year=1981|chapter=Kotani Kofun|title=Nara Prefecture Place names|series=[[Japanese Historical Place name Series]] 30|publisher=[[Heibonsha]]|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|Kotani Kofun (Heibonsha)|1981}}}} == External Links ==
